title: Sleep and Rest Promotion
subtitle: The nurse promotes sleep and rest — a dark, quiet, cool room, clustered care, a consistent bedtime routine, and nonpharmacologic measures before sleep medications.

patient.name: Sleep & Rest
patient.admitDate: The nurse promotes adequate sleep and rest using sleep hygiene and nonpharmacologic measures.

nursesNote: The nurse promotes sleep and rest by supporting good sleep hygiene and reducing barriers. A dark, quiet, cool room, a consistent sleep schedule, and a calming bedtime routine all help, while caffeine, nicotine, alcohol, large evening meals, screens, and long daytime naps interfere with sleep. In the hospital the nurse clusters care to allow uninterrupted rest, dims lights, lowers noise at night, manages pain, and offers toileting before bed. Daytime activity and relaxation measures (warm milk, quiet music, deep breathing) support nighttime sleep. The nurse assesses the cause of any insomnia — pain, anxiety, environment, or nocturia — and addresses it first, trying nonpharmacologic measures before giving a prescribed sleep aid. Sleep medications are used short-term because long-term use increases fall risk, especially in older adults, who normally have lighter sleep and more awakenings.
providerOrders: Provide a dark, quiet, cool environment for sleep
Cluster nursing care to reduce nighttime interruptions
Limit caffeine, nicotine, alcohol, and long daytime naps
Manage pain and offer toileting and a bedtime routine
Try nonpharmacologic measures before giving a sleep aid

opts.text: A dark, quiet, cool room promotes sleep
Clustering care reduces nighttime interruptions
Caffeine and nicotine can interfere with sleep

Managing pain supports better sleep

Daytime activity and exercise can improve nighttime sleep
Alcohol disrupts the quality of sleep

Clustering care to avoid waking the patient

A dark, quiet, cool room

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient reports they cannot sleep
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the cause (pain, anxiety, environment, nocturia)
Address comfort and the environment first
Offer nonpharmacologic measures (relaxation, warm milk)
Give a prescribed sleep aid if still needed
Reassess the patient's response

Assessing the cause of insomnia
Relying on nightly sedatives long-term for an older adult
